old_claim.note: Supply Chain Management Software supports manufacturing, deployment, commercialization, or operational scaling.

new_claim.note: Warehouse AMR deployments scale by integrating robot material-handling software with WMS order, inventory, and shipping workflows.

source_shape.source_locator: Sections explaining that Kiva MHS integrates with a client's WMS, receives orders from the WMS, and returns completed order notifications.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source describes Kiva as a mobile robotic fulfillment system and explains how its material-handling software interacts with warehouse management systems.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: This pins the operational scaling dependency to warehouse management systems rather than broad supply-chain management software, which is too wide for the warehouse AMR scope.

ontology_before: Warehouse AMRs depended on broad supply-chain management software, laundering an enterprise planning category into a warehouse-floor robotics integration dependency.
ontology_after: Warehouse AMRs depend commercially on warehouse management systems for order, inventory, and shipping workflow integration.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: warehouse_autonomous_mobile_robots no longer directly depends on supply_chain_management_software and now uses warehouse_management_systems for WMS integration.
would_reject_if: The node were rescoped from warehouse AMRs to enterprise supply-chain planning systems.
A source showed AMR warehouse deployment requires broad SCM software rather than WMS, order-management, ERP, or legacy warehouse interfaces.
A future source-specific node modeled Kiva MHS internals separately and replaced this WMS scaling edge.
short_reason: Kiva-style warehouse AMRs integrate with WMS workflows; broad SCM software is the wrong dependency level.
